There's a problem that's been facing restaurant owners for a long time. No shows. People who call make a reservation for a table and then don't appear. However, a restaurateur in Chicago, Gordon Sinclair, solved this problem by adding two words to what his receptionist says when taking a booking. She used to say, please call if you have to change or cancel your reservation. Now, Mr. Sinclair was a student of the influence process who knew about the principle of consistency and how it is activated by active public voluntary commitments. So what two words did he ask the receptionist to add to get people to call if they needed to back out? He had her say, will you please call if you have to change or cancel your reservation? And then to pause. No callers could fill the pause with an active public voluntary commitment by saying sure or glad to or of course. The result? Unannounced no show dropped from 30% to 10% that day and never went back up. Two small words, will you? But with outsized impact. Another instance of a small big in action. But let's consider another situation in which we can employ those same two magic words to produce a desirable outcome. Use your in charge of a team meeting at work in which you assign a particular task to each team member to complete fully before the next meeting. Don't let anyone out of that room until you've asked each person, will you be able to finish this task properly before our next scheduled meeting? Take pause. For those who say yes, by virtue of the principle of consistency you will have significantly increase the probability that they will return with a properly completed task.
